event: Early humans inhabit the area around Monaco, leaving traces of occupation in caves such as the Observatory Cave dating to the Lower Paleolithic.
Ligurian tribes settle the rocky coastal region that would become Monaco.
Greek colonists from Phocaea (via Massalia) establish trade contact along the coast and associate the harbour with the cult of Hercules Monoikos, giving Monaco its name.
Roman expansion into the region brings the area under the influence of Rome as part of the Maritime Alps.
With the fall of the Western Roman Empire, the region falls into instability and is contested by successive powers.
Emperor Frederick Barbarossa grants the Genoese authority over the coastline including Monaco.
Holy Roman Emperor Henry VI grants the territory of Monaco to the Republic of Genoa.
Genoese Ghibelline supporters begin building a fortress on the Rock of Monaco, founding the settlement that anchors the modern state.
Francesco Grimaldi, disguised as a Franciscan monk, seizes the fortress of Monaco for the Guelph faction, beginning the Grimaldi dynasty's association with the Rock.
Charles Grimaldi (Charles I) consolidates Grimaldi control over Monaco and is regarded as a founder of the principality.
The Grimaldi family secures definitive possession of Monaco after purchasing rights, establishing lasting sovereignty over the Rock.
France and the Duchy of Savoy formally recognise the independence of Monaco under Lambert Grimaldi.
King Louis XII of France confirms Monaco's independence and friendship, affirming Grimaldi sovereignty.
Augustin Grimaldi places Monaco under the protection of the Spanish Habsburg crown, beginning a long period of Spanish alliance.
Honoré II adopts the title of Prince of Monaco, formally elevating the realm to a principality.
By the Treaty of Péronne, Honoré II shifts Monaco's allegiance from Spain to France, accepting French protection while retaining sovereignty.
The male Grimaldi line ends with Antonio I, and the title passes through his daughter, with the Goyon de Matignon family adopting the Grimaldi name.
Revolutionary France annexes Monaco, abolishing the principality and renaming it Fort Hercule.
The first Treaty of Paris restores the Grimaldi dynasty and re-establishes the Principality of Monaco.
The Congress of Vienna places Monaco under the protection of the Kingdom of Sardinia.
The towns of Menton and Roquebrune revolt and break away from Monaco, declaring themselves free cities.
By a treaty with France, Prince Charles III cedes Menton and Roquebrune to France in exchange for recognition of Monaco's full independence.
Prince Charles III grants a concession to the Société des Bains de Mer, launching Monaco's casino and tourism economy.
The new district built around the casino is named Monte Carlo in honour of Prince Charles III.
Monaco abolishes direct income tax on residents, establishing its enduring reputation as a tax haven.
The opulent Monte Carlo Casino and the Salle Garnier opera house, designed by Charles Garnier, are completed.
Prince Albert I succeeds to the throne and becomes renowned as a pioneering oceanographer and patron of science.
The Oceanographic Museum of Monaco, founded by Prince Albert I, opens to the public in Monaco-Ville.
Prince Albert I grants Monaco its first constitution, transforming the absolute monarchy into a constitutional one.
The Franco-Monegasque Treaty places Monaco under limited French protection and stipulates that its policies align with French interests.
The first Monaco Grand Prix is held through the streets of Monte Carlo, founding one of motorsport's most iconic races.
Italian forces occupy Monaco during World War II, later replaced by German occupation in 1943.
Monaco is liberated as Allied forces advance along the French Riviera near the end of World War II.
Prince Rainier III ascends the throne and begins a transformative reign that modernises the principality.
Prince Rainier III marries American film star Grace Kelly, drawing global attention to Monaco.
A new constitution promulgated by Rainier III abolishes the death penalty, grants women the vote, and shares power with an elected National Council.
A fiscal dispute with France under Charles de Gaulle ends with an agreement taxing French nationals resident in Monaco.
Princess Grace dies from injuries sustained in a car accident on the corniche above Monaco.
Monaco is admitted to the United Nations as a full member state.
Monaco adopts the euro as its currency by agreement with the European Union, minting its own euro coins.
A revised succession treaty with France and an amended constitution ensure the Grimaldi dynasty's continuity even without a direct heir.
Monaco joins the Council of Europe, signalling its integration into wider European institutions.
Prince Albert II succeeds his father Rainier III, who died after a reign of more than fifty years.
Prince Albert II marries South African Olympic swimmer Charlene Wittstock in a widely celebrated royal wedding.
Princess Charlene gives birth to twins, Jacques and Gabriella, securing the Grimaldi line of succession.
Monaco signs agreements increasing financial transparency and tax-information exchange to meet international standards.
